[[en:Sukunabikona]] <!--enwiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[ca:Sukunabikona]] {{Commons category|Sukunabikona}} [[fr:Sukunabikona]] [[it:Sukuna-hikona]] [[ja:スクナビコナ]] [[ko:스쿠나비코나]] [[mni:ꯁꯨꯀꯨꯅꯕꯤꯀꯣꯅ]] [[pl:Sukunabikona]] [[pt:Sukunahikona]] [[simple:Sukunabikona]] [[sv:Suku-Na-Bikona]] [[zh:少彥名]] {{wikidata link|Q3275687}} [[Category:Agricultural gods]] [[Category:Alcohol gods]] [[Category:Amatsukami]] [[Category:Health deities]] [[Category:Health gods]] [[Category:Hot springs of Japan]] [[Category:Japanese gods]] [[Category:Japanese mythology]] [[Category:Magic gods]] [[Category:Pages with simple english wikipedia interwikis]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:Sake]] [[Category:Shinto kami]] [[Category:Shizuri Faith]] [[Category:Tutelary deities]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Pages with unclosed square brackets]] [[Category:Pages with unclosed curly braces]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ca:Sukunabikona]][[co:commons:Category:Sukunabikona]][[en:Sukunabikona]][[fr:Sukunabikona]][[it:Sukuna-hikona]][[ja:スクナビコナ]][[ko:스쿠나비코나]][[mni:ꯁꯨꯀꯨꯅꯕꯤꯀꯣꯅ]][[pl:Sukunabikona]][[pt:Sukunahikona]][[simple:Sukunabikona]][[sv:Suku-Na-Bikona]][[zh:少彥名]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ca:Sukunabikona]][[co:commons:Category:Sukunabikona]][[en:Sukunabikona]][[fr:Sukunabikona]][[it:Sukuna-hikona]][[ja:スクナビコナ]][[ko:스쿠나비코나]][[mni:ꯁꯨꯀꯨꯅꯕꯤꯀꯣꯅ]][[pl:Sukunabikona]][[pt:Sukunahikona]][[simple:Sukunabikona]][[sv:Suku-Na-Bikona]][[zh:少彥名]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ca:Sukunabikona]][[sn:commons:Category:Sukunabikona]][[en:Sukunabikona]][[fr:Sukunabikona]][[it:Sukuna-hikona]][[ja:スクナビコナ]][[ko:스쿠나비코나]][[mni:ꯁꯨꯀꯨꯅꯕꯤꯀꯣꯅ]][[pl:Sukunabikona]][[pt:Sukunahikona]][[simple:Sukunabikona]][[sv:Suku-Na-Bikona]][[zh:少彥名]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q3275687]] Wikidata name: Sukunabikona [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:34, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{short description|Kami of hot springs (onsen), agriculture, healing, magic and sake}} {{Infobox deity | type = Shinto | name = Sukunabikona | image =Sukuna-biko and Okuninushi.jpg; File:Sukuna-biko and Okuninushi.jpg| parents =[[Kamimusubi]]; {{ill|Takamimusubi|WD=Q125400}}| gender = {{ill|male|WD=Q6581097}} | venerated_in = {{ill|Shinto|WD=Q812767}} }} '''Sukunabikona''' or '''Sukuna bikona''' (少彦名神, also known as '''Sukuna-biko''', '''Sukuna-biko-na''', '''Sukuna hikona''') is the [[Shinto]] [[kami]] of the ''{{ill|Onsen|en|Onsen|WD=Q655311|lt=Onsen}}'' ({{ill|Hot spring|en|Hot spring|WD=Q177380|lt=Hot spring}}), agriculture, healing, magic, brewing [[:en:Sake (beverage)|sake]] and knowledge. His name means "the small lord of renown." He is often described as being a {{ill|Dwarf (folklore)|en|Dwarf (folklore)|WD=Q214045|lt=dwarf}} and is frequently paired with [[Ōkuninushi]].<ref name="auto">Handbook of Japanese Mythology by Michael Ashkenazi, ABC-CLIO, 2003</ref>
==Adventures with Ōkuninushi==
One day while Ōkuninushi was at {{ill|Miho Bay|en|Miho Bay|WD=Q6845312|lt=Miho Bay}}, he saw a small boat on the {{ill|Wind wave|en|Wind wave|WD=Q165848|lt=whitecap waves}}. The boat was made of a {{ill|Metaplexis|en|Metaplexis|WD=Q11692226|lt=Metaplexis}} pod. Inside was a small dwarf, no bigger than a thumb. Ōkuninushi picked him up, and Sukuna-biko-na bit him on the cheek. Ōkuninushi asked him his name, but he would not reply. Then a nearby toad said to bring Sukuna-biko-na to {{ill|Kuebiko|en|Kuebiko|WD=Q6441847|lt=Kuebiko}} the kami of agriculture, as the {{ill|Scarecrow|en|Scarecrow|WD=Q1990797|lt=Scarecrow}} god would know. When Kuebiko saw the dwarf, he said "That is Sukuna son of Kami-Musubi." [[Kamimusubi]] is part of the primordial creator trinity with [[Takamimusubi]] and [[Ame-no-Minakanushi]]. Ōkuninushi was shocked that such a small being could be the son of such a powerful [[:en:Creator goddess|Creator goddess]].
Ōkuninushi brought Sukuna-biko-na to Kami-Musubi, who said that yes, he was her son, and that he fell out of her fingers. Ōkuninushi was on a quest to continue the creation of the {{ill|Names of Japan|en|Names of Japan|WD=Q13423964|lt=Central Reed Plain}} and unite the people. She decreed that the two would work together to help finish building {{ill|Izumo Province|en|Izumo Province|WD=Q582846|lt=Izumo}}, and they joined forces to help build the land.<ref>Kojiki edited by Donald L. Philippi, Princeton University Press, 2015. {{ISBN|9780691622071}}</ref>
Tradition holds that Sukuna-biko-na left this world at {{ill|Awaji Island|en|Awaji Island|WD=Q504373|lt=Awaji Island}} by climbing a grain of {{ill|Millet|en|Millet|WD=Q259438|lt=Millet}}, which then dipped under his weight and rebounded, flinging him into {{ill|Ne-no-kuni|en|Ne-no-kuni|WD=Q7555425|lt=Tokoyo no Kuni}}.<ref name="auto2">Shinto, the Way of the Gods by William George Aston, Longmans Green, 1905</ref>
As part of his quest to help Ōkuninushi complete construction of the land, Sukuna-biko-na invented medicines and cures for illnesses and diseases, including magical spells for protection. In addition to his other domains, he is a master of magic and wizardry. His ascension into space, rather than a natural death, makes him a {{ill|Marebito|en|Marebito|WD=Q6758746|lt=Marebito}}.<ref name="auto"/>
==As Deity of Hot Springs== On one of their adventures, Sukuna became ill. They went to {{ill|Dōgo Onsen|en|Dōgo Onsen|WD=Q1037748|lt=the Dōgo hot springs}}. Ōkuninushi put Sukuna-biko in the hot spring water to revive him. Sukuna hikona then took a nap. When he awoke, he was cured, and Sukuna danced on a stone. His footprints left an impression on the rock, known as Tamanoishi, which remains in Dogo Onsen today north of the main building. [[File:Dōgo_Onsen_in_2010-9-6_No,9.JPG|thumb|Tamanoishi at Dogo Onsen]]
<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://taiken.co/single/spirited-away-at-dogo-onsen/|title=Spirited Away at Dogo Onsen|website=Taiken Japan|access-date=2019-09-24|archive-date=2020-10-22|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201022042410/https://taiken.co/single/spirited-away-at-dogo-onsen/|url-status=dead}}</ref><ref name="auto3">A Handbook for Travellers in Japan, including the Whole Empire from Yezo to Formosa, by Basil Hall Chamberlain & W.B. Mason, 1901</ref> The ability for the natural hot spring to cure a god bestowed the waters, and the land itself from which they sprung, a divine importance.
He is known as an immigrant deity, in that he came from across the sea and then departed the land.<ref name="auto4"> Shotoku: Ethnicity, Ritual, and Violence in the Japanese Buddhist Tradition by Michael I. Como, Oxford University Press, 2008</ref>
==As Inventor of Sake== Some sources credit him as well with the invention of [[Sake]]. He taught the people how to brew the beverage from {{ill|Rice|en|Rice|WD=Q5090|lt=Rice}}.<ref>A History of the Japanese People from the Earliest Times to the End of the Meiji Era by Francis Brinkley and Kikuchi Dairoku, 1912</ref>
In the ''Kojiki'', {{ill|Empress Jingū|en|Empress Jingū|WD=Q232803|lt=Empress Jingū}} gives a banquet toast crediting the deity Sukuna with the creation of Sake:
<poem> This fine liquor Is not my fine liquor. The potion-master Who dwells in the Everworld (tokoyo) The rock-standing Deity Sukuna With godly blessing, Blessing in frenzy, With abundant blessing Blessing around and around Came and offered This fine liquor: Drink unsparingly ''Sa! Sa!''
Thereupon Takeuchi no Sukune no Mikoto replied on behalf of the prince:
He who brewed This fine liquor-was it because He took his drum And standing it up like a mortar, Singing while he brewed Did the brewing of the beer Dancing while he brewed Did the brewing of the beer, That this fine liquor, Fine liquor, Makes me feel so extra good? ''Sa! Sa!'' </poem>
Due to Sukuna bikona's gift of liquor to the people, he is sometimes paired with the goddess of food {{ill|Ukemochi|en|Ukemochi|WD=Q2280323|lt=Ukemochi}}. The reference to the standing stone in the song refers to his worship as a sacred stone. Both Sukuna bikona and Ohona-mochi are enshrined as sacred stones in a temple in Noto Province.<ref name="auto2"/><ref name="auto4"/>
==Shrines== In Shinto, it is believed that the god resides within a sacred object housed at the shrine. It is possible for a deity to be in more than one place at a time, as such a Kami may be housed at many shrines, as well as being omnipresent and omniscient.
Sukuna Hikona is enshrined at, {{ill|Ōarai, Ibaraki|en|Ōarai, Ibaraki|WD=Q1349185|lt=Oarai Isosaki-jinja}}. In the Montoku Jitsuroku, a book of Japanese history, Ōkuninushi and Sukuna Hikona descended in 856, proclaiming that they had returned to help the people of the land and a shrine was built to honor their arrival.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.pref.ibaraki.jp/bugai/kokusai/tabunka/en/oneday/20131220-oaraiisosaki.html|title=Oarai Isosaki Shrine|website=茨城県|access-date=2019-09-24|archive-date=2019-09-24|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190924223136/http://www.pref.ibaraki.jp/bugai/kokusai/tabunka/en/oneday/20131220-oaraiisosaki.html|url-status=dead}}</ref>
[[File:Sukunahikona-Shrine-Osaka.jpg|thumb|Sukunahikona shrine at Osaka]]
He is one of the Shinto deities enshrined at {{ill|Mount Mitake (Tokyo)|en|Mount Mitake (Tokyo)|WD=Q1432565|lt=Mt. Mitake}}<ref name="auto3"/>
In Noto Province there are Stone Idols said to represent Sukuna bikona and Ohana-mochi.<ref name="auto2"/>
The [[少彦名神社 (大洲市)|Sukuna hikona shrine]] in Ozu city, Ehime prefecture was registered by the World Monument Foundation in 2014, "Monument List of the Endangered World." [[File:Sukunahikona_haiden.JPG|thumb|Sukunahikona haiden in Ozu city.]]
He is enshrined at Awashima in Wakayama. Empress Jingu, while on a journey over the sea, encountered a storm and her ship almost sank. She landed on Awashima island and discovered a shrine for Sukuna Hikona. She prayed for protection and promised she would build a larger shrine in his honor if he protected her. She was granted safe passage, and fulfilled her promise. Empress Jingu was then worshipped alongside Sukuna and Okuniushi. In one version, Harisaijo, Amaterasu's daughter, is exiled to the island of Awashima for having a woman's ailment. She encounters Sukuna hino's shrine and vows to help women.<ref>Encyclopedia of Spirits: The Ultimate Guide to the Magic of Fairies, Genies, Demons, Ghosts, Gods & Goddesses by Judika Illes, 2009, HarperOne First Edition</ref>
Empress Jingu is so taken by Sukuna Hikona that she carves a doll in his likeness, a hina-ningyō. This may be the origin of the Hina doll.<ref name="auto1">The Sea and the Sacred in Japan: Aspects of Maritime Religion by Fabio Rambelli, Bloomsbury Publishing 2018</ref>
==As Kami of Healing and Magic== Sukuna Hikona, as a kami of healing, is credited with the invention of cures for several diseases of humans and animals. He was invoked for protection against "creeping things" and "calamities." Creeping things refers to insects, snakes, and other pests that could damage crops. As a god of agriculture, farmers would pray to him for the protection of their crops. Calamities is a euphemism for unseen or spiritual forces. As a Marebito and a son of Kamimusubi, Sukuna bikona was omniscient and proficient in magic. He would offer protection in the way of spells and incantations.<ref>Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697, Volume 1</ref>
[[File:Sukunahikona-Shrine-Prayer-tablet.jpg|thumb|Prayer tablet (rear) of the Sukunahikona-Shrine in Osaka (Japan) showing the medicinal deities Sukunahikona (Japan) and Shennong (China)]]
For his interaction with Empress Jingu, who was pregnant at the time of her voyage, he is also credited with helping women's health, women's reproductive ailments, fertility, and safe childbirth.<ref name="auto1"/>
==Eponymy==
The asteroid {{ill|10725 Sukunabikona|en|10725 Sukunabikona|WD=Q2627958|lt=10725 Sukunabikona}} derives its name from the kami.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://ssd.jpl.nasa.gov/sbdb.cgi?sstr=10725#content|title=JPL Small-Body Database Browser|website=ssd.jpl.nasa.gov}}</ref>

Philippi, Princeton University Press, 2015. {{ISBN|9780691622071}}</ref>

In addition to his other domains, he is a master of magic and wizardry. His ascension into space, rather than a natural death, makes him a {{ill|Marebito|en|Marebito|WD=Q6758746|lt=Marebito}}.<ref name="auto"/>
==As Deity of Hot Springs== On one of their adventures, Sukuna became ill. They went to {{ill|Dōgo Onsen|en|Dōgo Onsen|WD=Q1037748|lt=the Dōgo hot springs}}. Ōkuninushi put Sukuna-biko in the hot spring water to revive him. Sukuna hikona then took a nap. When he awoke, he was cured, and Sukuna danced on a stone. His footprints left an impression on the rock, known as Tamanoishi, which remains in Dogo Onsen today north of the main building. [[File:Dōgo_Onsen_in_2010-9-6_No,9.JPG|thumb|Tamanoishi at Dogo Onsen]]
<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://taiken.co/single/spirited-away-at-dogo-onsen/|title=Spirited Away at Dogo Onsen|website=Taiken Japan|access-date=2019-09-24|archive-date=2020-10-22|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201022042410/https://taiken.co/single/spirited-away-at-dogo-onsen/|url-status=dead}}</ref><ref name="auto3">A Handbook for Travellers in Japan, including the Whole Empire from Yezo to Formosa, by Basil Hall Chamberlain & W.B. Mason, 1901</ref> The ability for the natural hot spring to cure a god bestowed the waters, and the land itself from which they sprung, a divine importance.
He is known as an immigrant deity, in that he came from across the sea and then departed the land.<ref name="auto4"> Shotoku: Ethnicity, Ritual, and Violence in the Japanese Buddhist Tradition by Michael I. Como, Oxford University Press, 2008</ref>
==As Inventor of Sake== Some sources credit him as well with the invention of [[Sake]]. He taught the people how to brew the beverage from {{ill|Rice|en|Rice|WD=Q5090|lt=Rice}}.<ref>A History of the Japanese People from the Earliest Times to the End of the Meiji Era by Francis Brinkley and Kikuchi Dairoku, 1912</ref>
In the ''Kojiki'', {{ill|Empress Jingū|en|Empress Jingū|WD=Q232803|lt=Empress Jingū}} gives a banquet toast crediting the deity Sukuna with the creation of Sake:
<poem> This fine liquor Is not my fine liquor. The potion-master Who dwells in the Everworld (tokoyo) The rock-standing Deity Sukuna With godly blessing, Blessing in frenzy, With abundant blessing Blessing around and around Came and offered This fine liquor: Drink unsparingly ''Sa! Sa!''
Thereupon Takeuchi no Sukune no Mikoto replied on behalf of the prince:
He who brewed This fine liquor-was it because He took his drum And standing it up like a mortar, Singing while he brewed Did the brewing of the beer Dancing while he brewed Did the brewing of the beer, That this fine liquor, Fine liquor, Makes me feel so extra good? ''Sa! Sa!'' </poem>
Due to Sukuna bikona's gift of liquor to the people, he is sometimes paired with the goddess of food {{ill|Ukemochi|en|Ukemochi|WD=Q2280323|lt=Ukemochi}}. The reference to the standing stone in the song refers to his worship as a sacred stone. Both Sukuna bikona and Ohona-mochi are enshrined as sacred stones in a temple in Noto Province.<ref name="auto2"/><ref name="auto4"/>
==Shrines== In Shinto, it is believed that the god resides within a sacred object housed at the shrine. It is possible for a deity to be in more than one place at a time, as such a Kami may be housed at many shrines, as well as being omnipresent and omniscient.
Sukuna Hikona is enshrined at, {{ill|Ōarai, Ibaraki|en|Ōarai, Ibaraki|WD=Q1349185|lt=Oarai Isosaki-jinja}}. In the Montoku Jitsuroku, a book of Japanese history, Ōkuninushi and Sukuna Hikona descended in 856, proclaiming that they had returned to help the people of the land and a shrine was built to honor their arrival.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.pref.ibaraki.jp/bugai/kokusai/tabunka/en/oneday/20131220-oaraiisosaki.html|title=Oarai Isosaki Shrine|website=茨城県|access-date=2019-09-24|archive-date=2019-09-24|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190924223136/http://www.pref.ibaraki.jp/bugai/kokusai/tabunka/en/oneday/20131220-oaraiisosaki.html|url-status=dead}}</ref>
[[File:Sukunahikona-Shrine-Osaka.jpg|thumb|Sukunahikona shrine at Osaka]]
He is one of the Shinto deities enshrined at {{ill|Mount Mitake (Tokyo)|en|Mount Mitake (Tokyo)|WD=Q1432565|lt=Mt. Mitake}}<ref name="auto3"/>
In Noto Province there are Stone Idols said to represent Sukuna bikona and Ohana-mochi.<ref name="auto2"/>
The [[少彦名神社 (大洲市)|Sukuna hikona shrine]] in Ozu city, Ehime prefecture was registered by the World Monument Foundation in 2014, "Monument List of the Endangered World." [[File:Sukunahikona_haiden.JPG|thumb|Sukunahikona haiden in Ozu city.]]
He is enshrined at Awashima in Wakayama. Empress Jingu, while on a journey over the sea, encountered a storm and her ship almost sank. She landed on Awashima island and discovered a shrine for Sukuna Hikona. She prayed for protection and promised she would build a larger shrine in his honor if he protected her. She was granted safe passage, and fulfilled her promise. Empress Jingu was then worshipped alongside Sukuna and Okuniushi. In one version, Harisaijo, Amaterasu's daughter, is exiled to the island of Awashima for having a woman's ailment. She encounters Sukuna hino's shrine and vows to help women.<ref>Encyclopedia of Spirits: The Ultimate Guide to the Magic of Fairies, Genies, Demons, Ghosts, Gods & Goddesses by Judika Illes, 2009, HarperOne First Edition</ref>
Empress Jingu is so taken by Sukuna Hikona that she carves a doll in his likeness, a hina-ningyō. This may be the origin of the Hina doll.<ref name="auto1">The Sea and the Sacred in Japan: Aspects of Maritime Religion by Fabio Rambelli, Bloomsbury Publishing 2018</ref>
==As Kami of Healing and Magic== Sukuna Hikona, as a kami of healing, is credited with the invention of cures for several diseases of humans and animals. He was invoked for protection against "creeping things" and "calamities." Creeping things refers to insects, snakes, and other pests that could damage crops. As a god of agriculture, farmers would pray to him for the protection of their crops. Calamities is a euphemism for unseen or spiritual forces. As a Marebito and a son of Kamimusubi, Sukuna bikona was omniscient and proficient in magic. He would offer protection in the way of spells and incantations.<ref>Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697, Volume 1</ref>
[[File:Sukunahikona-Shrine-Prayer-tablet.jpg|thumb|Prayer tablet (rear) of the Sukunahikona-Shrine in Osaka (Japan) showing the medicinal deities Sukunahikona (Japan) and Shennong (China)]]
For his interaction with Empress Jingu, who was pregnant at the time of her voyage, he is also credited with helping women's health, women's reproductive ailments, fertility, and safe childbirth.<ref name="auto1"/>
==Eponymy==
The asteroid {{ill|10725 Sukunabikona|en|10725 Sukunabikona|WD=Q2627958|lt=10725 Sukunabikona}} derives its name from the kami.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://ssd.jpl.nasa.gov/sbdb.cgi?sstr=10725#content|title=JPL Small-Body Database Browser|website=ssd.jpl.nasa.gov}}</ref>

{{short description|Kami of hot springs (onsen), agriculture, healing, magic and sake}} {{Infobox deity | type = Shinto | name = Sukunabikona | image =Sukuna-biko and Okuninushi.jpg; File:Sukuna-biko and Okuninushi.jpg| parents =[[Kamimusubi]]; {{ill|Takamimusubi|WD=Q125400}}| gender = {{ill|male|WD=Q6581097}} | venerated_in = {{ill|Shinto|WD=Q812767}} }} '''Sukunabikona''' or '''Sukuna bikona''' (少彦名神, also known as '''Sukuna-biko''', '''Sukuna-biko-na''', '''Sukuna hikona''') is the [[Shinto]] [[kami]] of the ''{{ill|Onsen|en|Onsen|WD=Q655311|lt=Onsen}}'' ({{ill|Hot spring|en|Hot spring|WD=Q177380|lt=Hot spring}}), agriculture, healing, magic, brewing [[:en:Sake (beverage)|sake]] and knowledge. His name means "the small lord of renown." He is often described as being a {{ill|Dwarf (folklore)|en|Dwarf (folklore)|WD=Q214045|lt=dwarf}} and is frequently paired with [[Ōkuninushi]].<ref name="auto">Handbook of Japanese Mythology by Michael Ashkenazi, ABC-CLIO, 2003</ref>
==Adventures with Ōkuninushi==
One day while Ōkuninushi was at {{ill|Miho Bay|en|Miho Bay|WD=Q6845312|lt=Miho Bay}}, he saw a small boat on the {{ill|Wind wave|en|Wind wave|WD=Q165848|lt=whitecap waves}}. The boat was made of a {{ill|Metaplexis|en|Metaplexis|WD=Q11692226|lt=Metaplexis}} pod. Inside was a small dwarf, no bigger than a thumb. Ōkuninushi picked him up, and Sukuna-biko-na bit him on the cheek. Ōkuninushi asked him his name, but he would not reply. Then a nearby toad said to bring Sukuna-biko-na to {{ill|Kuebiko|en|Kuebiko|WD=Q6441847|lt=Kuebiko}} the kami of agriculture, as the {{ill|Scarecrow|en|Scarecrow|WD=Q1990797|lt=Scarecrow}} god would know. When Kuebiko saw the dwarf, he said "That is Sukuna son of Kami-Musubi." [[Kamimusubi]] is part of the primordial creator trinity with [[Takamimusubi]] and [[Ame-no-Minakanushi]]. Ōkuninushi was shocked that such a small being could be the son of such a powerful [[:en:Creator goddess|Creator goddess]].
Ōkuninushi brought Sukuna-biko-na to Kami-Musubi, who said that yes, he was her son, and that he fell out of her fingers. Ōkuninushi was on a quest to continue the creation of the {{ill|Names of Japan|en|Names of Japan|WD=Q13423964|lt=Central Reed Plain}} and unite the people. She decreed that the two would work together to help finish building {{ill|Izumo Province|en|Izumo Province|WD=Q582846|lt=Izumo}}, and they joined forces to help build the land.<ref>Kojiki edited by Donald L. Philippi, Princeton University Press, 2015. {{ISBN|9780691622071}}</ref>
Tradition holds that Sukuna-biko-na left this world at {{ill|Awaji Island|en|Awaji Island|WD=Q504373|lt=Awaji Island}} by climbing a grain of {{ill|Millet|en|Millet|WD=Q259438|lt=Millet}}, which then dipped under his weight and rebounded, flinging him into {{ill|Ne-no-kuni|en|Ne-no-kuni|WD=Q7555425|lt=Tokoyo no Kuni}}.<ref name="auto2">Shinto, the Way of the Gods by William George Aston, Longmans Green, 1905</ref>
As part of his quest to help Ōkuninushi complete construction of the land, Sukuna-biko-na invented medicines and cures for illnesses and diseases, including magical spells for protection. In addition to his other domains, he is a master of magic and wizardry. His ascension into space, rather than a natural death, makes him a {{ill|Marebito|en|Marebito|WD=Q6758746|lt=Marebito}}.<ref name="auto"/>
==As Deity of Hot Springs== On one of their adventures, Sukuna became ill. They went to {{ill|Dōgo Onsen|en|Dōgo Onsen|WD=Q1037748|lt=the Dōgo hot springs}}. Ōkuninushi put Sukuna-biko in the hot spring water to revive him. Sukuna hikona then took a nap. When he awoke, he was cured, and Sukuna danced on a stone. His footprints left an impression on the rock, known as Tamanoishi, which remains in Dogo Onsen today north of the main building. [[File:Dōgo_Onsen_in_2010-9-6_No,9.JPG|thumb|Tamanoishi at Dogo Onsen]]
<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://taiken.co/single/spirited-away-at-dogo-onsen/|title=Spirited Away at Dogo Onsen|website=Taiken Japan|access-date=2019-09-24|archive-date=2020-10-22|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201022042410/https://taiken.co/single/spirited-away-at-dogo-onsen/|url-status=dead}}</ref><ref name="auto3">A Handbook for Travellers in Japan, including the Whole Empire from Yezo to Formosa, by Basil Hall Chamberlain & W.B. Mason, 1901</ref> The ability for the natural hot spring to cure a god bestowed the waters, and the land itself from which they sprung, a divine importance.
He is known as an immigrant deity, in that he came from across the sea and then departed the land.<ref name="auto4"> Shotoku: Ethnicity, Ritual, and Violence in the Japanese Buddhist Tradition by Michael I. Como, Oxford University Press, 2008</ref>
==As Inventor of Sake== Some sources credit him as well with the invention of [[Sake]]. He taught the people how to brew the beverage from {{ill|Rice|en|Rice|WD=Q5090|lt=Rice}}.<ref>A History of the Japanese People from the Earliest Times to the End of the Meiji Era by Francis Brinkley and Kikuchi Dairoku, 1912</ref>
In the ''Kojiki'', {{ill|Empress Jingū|en|Empress Jingū|WD=Q232803|lt=Empress Jingū}} gives a banquet toast crediting the deity Sukuna with the creation of Sake:
<poem> This fine liquor Is not my fine liquor. The potion-master Who dwells in the Everworld (tokoyo) The rock-standing Deity Sukuna With godly blessing, Blessing in frenzy, With abundant blessing Blessing around and around Came and offered This fine liquor: Drink unsparingly ''Sa! Sa!''
Thereupon Takeuchi no Sukune no Mikoto replied on behalf of the prince:
He who brewed This fine liquor-was it because He took his drum And standing it up like a mortar, Singing while he brewed Did the brewing of the beer Dancing while he brewed Did the brewing of the beer, That this fine liquor, Fine liquor, Makes me feel so extra good? ''Sa! Sa!'' </poem>
Due to Sukuna bikona's gift of liquor to the people, he is sometimes paired with the goddess of food {{ill|Ukemochi|en|Ukemochi|WD=Q2280323|lt=Ukemochi}}. The reference to the standing stone in the song refers to his worship as a sacred stone. Both Sukuna bikona and Ohona-mochi are enshrined as sacred stones in a temple in Noto Province.<ref name="auto2"/><ref name="auto4"/>
==Shrines== In Shinto, it is believed that the god resides within a sacred object housed at the shrine. It is possible for a deity to be in more than one place at a time, as such a Kami may be housed at many shrines, as well as being omnipresent and omniscient.

[[File:Sukunahikona-Shrine-Osaka.jpg|thumb|Sukunahikona shrine at Osaka]]
He is one of the Shinto deities enshrined at {{ill|Mount Mitake (Tokyo)|en|Mount Mitake (Tokyo)|WD=Q1432565|lt=Mt. Mitake}}<ref name="auto3"/>
In Noto Province there are Stone Idols said to represent Sukuna bikona and Ohana-mochi.<ref name="auto2"/>
The [[少彦名神社 (大洲市)|Sukuna hikona shrine]] in Ozu city, Ehime prefecture was registered by the World Monument Foundation in 2014, "Monument List of the Endangered World." [[File:Sukunahikona_haiden.JPG|thumb|Sukunahikona haiden in Ozu city.]]
He is enshrined at Awashima in Wakayama. Empress Jingu, while on a journey over the sea, encountered a storm and her ship almost sank. She landed on Awashima island and discovered a shrine for Sukuna Hikona. She prayed for protection and promised she would build a larger shrine in his honor if he protected her. She was granted safe passage, and fulfilled her promise. Empress Jingu was then worshipped alongside Sukuna and Okuniushi. In one version, Harisaijo, Amaterasu's daughter, is exiled to the island of Awashima for having a woman's ailment. She encounters Sukuna hino's shrine and vows to help women.<ref>Encyclopedia of Spirits: The Ultimate Guide to the Magic of Fairies, Genies, Demons, Ghosts, Gods & Goddesses by Judika Illes, 2009, HarperOne First Edition</ref>
Empress Jingu is so taken by Sukuna Hikona that she carves a doll in his likeness, a hina-ningyō. This may be the origin of the Hina doll.<ref name="auto1">The Sea and the Sacred in Japan: Aspects of Maritime Religion by Fabio Rambelli, Bloomsbury Publishing 2018</ref>

He was invoked for protection against "creeping things" and "calamities." Creeping things refers to insects, snakes, and other pests that could damage crops. As a god of agriculture, farmers would pray to him for the protection of their crops. Calamities is a euphemism for unseen or spiritual forces. As a Marebito and a son of Kamimusubi, Sukuna bikona was omniscient and proficient in magic. He would offer protection in the way of spells and incantations.<ref>Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697, Volume 1</ref>
[[File:Sukunahikona-Shrine-Prayer-tablet.jpg|thumb|Prayer tablet (rear) of the Sukunahikona-Shrine in Osaka (Japan) showing the medicinal deities Sukunahikona (Japan) and Shennong (China)]]
For his interaction with Empress Jingu, who was pregnant at the time of her voyage, he is also credited with helping women's health, women's reproductive ailments, fertility, and safe childbirth.<ref name="auto1"/>

The boat was made of a {{ill|Metaplexis|en|Metaplexis|WD=Q11692226|lt=Metaplexis|1=Metaplexis}} pod. Inside was a small dwarf, no bigger than a thumb. Ōkuninushi picked him up, and Sukuna-biko-na bit him on the cheek. Ōkuninushi asked him his name, but he would not reply. Then a nearby toad said to bring Sukuna-biko-na to {{ill|Kuebiko|en|Kuebiko|WD=Q6441847|lt=Kuebiko|1=Kuebiko}} the kami of agriculture, as the {{ill|Scarecrow|en|Scarecrow|WD=Q1990797|lt=Scarecrow|1=Scarecrow}} god would know. When Kuebiko saw the dwarf, he said "That is Sukuna son of Kami-Musubi." [[Kamimusubi]] is part of the primordial creator trinity with [[Takamimusubi]] and [[Ame-no-Minakanushi]]. Ōkuninushi was shocked that such a small being could be the son of such a powerful [[:en:Creator goddess|Creator goddess]].
Ōkuninushi brought Sukuna-biko-na to Kami-Musubi, who said that yes, he was her son, and that he fell out of her fingers. Ōkuninushi was on a quest to continue the creation of the {{ill|Names of Japan|en|Names of Japan|WD=Q13423964|lt=Central Reed Plain|1=Names of Japan}} and unite the people. She decreed that the two would work together to help finish building {{ill|Izumo Province|en|Izumo Province|WD=Q582846|lt=Izumo|1=Izumo Province}}, and they joined forces to help build the land.<ref>Kojiki edited by Donald L. Philippi, Princeton University Press, 2015. {{ISBN|9780691622071}}</ref>
Tradition holds that Sukuna-biko-na left this world at {{ill|Awaji Island|en|Awaji Island|WD=Q504373|lt=Awaji Island|1=Awaji Island}} by climbing a grain of {{ill|Millet|en|Millet|WD=Q259438|lt=Millet|1=Millet}}, which then dipped under his weight and rebounded, flinging him into {{ill|Ne-no-kuni|en|Ne-no-kuni|WD=Q7555425|lt=Tokoyo no Kuni|1=Ne-no-kuni}}.<ref name="auto2">Shinto, the Way of the Gods by William George Aston, Longmans Green, 1905</ref>
As part of his quest to help Ōkuninushi complete construction of the land, Sukuna-biko-na invented medicines and cures for illnesses and diseases, including magical spells for protection. In addition to his other domains, he is a master of magic and wizardry. His ascension into space, rather than a natural death, makes him a {{ill|Marebito|en|Marebito|WD=Q6758746|lt=Marebito|1=Marebito}}.<ref name="auto"/>
==As Deity of Hot Springs== On one of their adventures, Sukuna became ill. They went to {{ill|Dōgo Onsen|en|Dōgo Onsen|WD=Q1037748|lt=the Dōgo hot springs|1=Dōgo Onsen}}. Ōkuninushi put Sukuna-biko in the hot spring water to revive him. Sukuna hikona then took a nap. When he awoke, he was cured, and Sukuna danced on a stone. His footprints left an impression on the rock, known as Tamanoishi, which remains in Dogo Onsen today north of the main building. [[File:Dōgo_Onsen_in_2010-9-6_No,9.JPG|thumb|Tamanoishi at Dogo Onsen]]
<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://taiken.co/single/spirited-away-at-dogo-onsen/|title=Spirited Away at Dogo Onsen|website=Taiken Japan|access-date=2019-09-24|archive-date=2020-10-22|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201022042410/https://taiken.co/single/spirited-away-at-dogo-onsen/|url-status=dead}}</ref><ref name="auto3">A Handbook for Travellers in Japan, including the Whole Empire from Yezo to Formosa, by Basil Hall Chamberlain & W.B. Mason, 1901</ref> The ability for the natural hot spring to cure a god bestowed the waters, and the land itself from which they sprung, a divine importance.
He is known as an immigrant deity, in that he came from across the sea and then departed the land.<ref name="auto4"> Shotoku: Ethnicity, Ritual, and Violence in the Japanese Buddhist Tradition by Michael I. Como, Oxford University Press, 2008</ref>
==As Inventor of Sake== Some sources credit him as well with the invention of [[Sake]]. He taught the people how to brew the beverage from {{ill|Rice|en|Rice|WD=Q5090|lt=Rice|1=Rice}}.<ref>A History of the Japanese People from the Earliest Times to the End of the Meiji Era by Francis Brinkley and Kikuchi Dairoku, 1912</ref>
In the ''Kojiki'', {{ill|Empress Jingū|en|Empress Jingū|WD=Q232803|lt=Empress Jingū|1=Empress Jingū}} gives a banquet toast crediting the deity Sukuna with the creation of Sake:
<poem> This fine liquor Is not my fine liquor. The potion-master Who dwells in the Everworld (tokoyo) The rock-standing Deity Sukuna With godly blessing, Blessing in frenzy, With abundant blessing Blessing around and around Came and offered This fine liquor: Drink unsparingly ''Sa! Sa!''
Thereupon Takeuchi no Sukune no Mikoto replied on behalf of the prince:
He who brewed This fine liquor-was it because He took his drum And standing it up like a mortar, Singing while he brewed Did the brewing of the beer Dancing while he brewed Did the brewing of the beer, That this fine liquor, Fine liquor, Makes me feel so extra good? ''Sa! Sa!'' </poem>
Due to Sukuna bikona's gift of liquor to the people, he is sometimes paired with the goddess of food {{ill|Ukemochi|en|Ukemochi|WD=Q2280323|lt=Ukemochi|1=Ukemochi}}. The reference to the standing stone in the song refers to his worship as a sacred stone. Both Sukuna bikona and Ohona-mochi are enshrined as sacred stones in a temple in Noto Province.<ref name="auto2"/><ref name="auto4"/>
==Shrines== In Shinto, it is believed that the god resides within a sacred object housed at the shrine. It is possible for a deity to be in more than one place at a time, as such a Kami may be housed at many shrines, as well as being omnipresent and omniscient.
Sukuna Hikona is enshrined at, {{ill|Ōarai, Ibaraki|en|Ōarai, Ibaraki|WD=Q1349185|lt=Oarai Isosaki-jinja|1=Ōarai, Ibaraki}}. In the Montoku Jitsuroku, a book of Japanese history, Ōkuninushi and Sukuna Hikona descended in 856, proclaiming that they had returned to help the people of the land and a shrine was built to honor their arrival.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.pref.ibaraki.jp/bugai/kokusai/tabunka/en/oneday/20131220-oaraiisosaki.html|title=Oarai Isosaki Shrine|website=茨城県|access-date=2019-09-24|archive-date=2019-09-24|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190924223136/http://www.pref.ibaraki.jp/bugai/kokusai/tabunka/en/oneday/20131220-oaraiisosaki.html|url-status=dead}}</ref>
[[File:Sukunahikona-Shrine-Osaka.jpg|thumb|Sukunahikona shrine at Osaka]]
He is one of the Shinto deities enshrined at {{ill|Mount Mitake (Tokyo)|en|Mount Mitake (Tokyo)|WD=Q1432565|lt=Mt. Mitake|1=Mount Mitake (Tokyo)}}<ref name="auto3"/>
In Noto Province there are Stone Idols said to represent Sukuna bikona and Ohana-mochi.<ref name="auto2"/>
The [[少彦名神社 (大洲市)|Sukuna hikona shrine]] in Ozu city, Ehime prefecture was registered by the World Monument Foundation in 2014, "Monument List of the Endangered World." [[File:Sukunahikona_haiden.JPG|thumb|Sukunahikona haiden in Ozu city.]]
He is enshrined at Awashima in Wakayama. Empress Jingu, while on a journey over the sea, encountered a storm and her ship almost sank. She landed on Awashima island and discovered a shrine for Sukuna Hikona. She prayed for protection and promised she would build a larger shrine in his honor if he protected her. She was granted safe passage, and fulfilled her promise. Empress Jingu was then worshipped alongside Sukuna and Okuniushi. In one version, Harisaijo, Amaterasu's daughter, is exiled to the island of Awashima for having a woman's ailment. She encounters Sukuna hino's shrine and vows to help women.<ref>Encyclopedia of Spirits: The Ultimate Guide to the Magic of Fairies, Genies, Demons, Ghosts, Gods & Goddesses by Judika Illes, 2009, HarperOne First Edition</ref>
Empress Jingu is so taken by Sukuna Hikona that she carves a doll in his likeness, a hina-ningyō. This may be the origin of the Hina doll.<ref name="auto1">The Sea and the Sacred in Japan: Aspects of Maritime Religion by Fabio Rambelli, Bloomsbury Publishing 2018</ref>
==As Kami of Healing and Magic== Sukuna Hikona, as a kami of healing, is credited with the invention of cures for several diseases of humans and animals. He was invoked for protection against "creeping things" and "calamities." Creeping things refers to insects, snakes, and other pests that could damage crops. As a god of agriculture, farmers would pray to him for the protection of their crops. Calamities is a euphemism for unseen or spiritual forces. As a Marebito and a son of Kamimusubi, Sukuna bikona was omniscient and proficient in magic. He would offer protection in the way of spells and incantations.<ref>Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697, Volume 1</ref>
[[File:Sukunahikona-Shrine-Prayer-tablet.jpg|thumb|Prayer tablet (rear) of the Sukunahikona-Shrine in Osaka (Japan) showing the medicinal deities Sukunahikona (Japan) and Shennong (China)]]
For his interaction with Empress Jingu, who was pregnant at the time of her voyage, he is also credited with helping women's health, women's reproductive ailments, fertility, and safe childbirth.<ref name="auto1"/>
==Eponymy==
The asteroid {{ill|10725 Sukunabikona|en|10725 Sukunabikona|WD=Q2627958|lt=10725 Sukunabikona|1=10725 Sukunabikona}} derives its name from the kami.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://ssd.jpl.nasa.gov/sbdb.cgi?sstr=10725#content|title=JPL Small-Body Database Browser|website=ssd.jpl.nasa.gov}}</ref>

{{short description|Kami of hot springs (onsen), agriculture, healing, magic and sake}} {{Infobox deity | type = Shinto | name = Sukunabikona | image =Sukuna-biko and Okuninushi.jpg; File:Sukuna-biko and Okuninushi.jpg| parents =[[Kamimusubi]]; {{ill|Takamimusubi|WD=Q125400|1=Takamimusubi}}| gender = {{ill|male|WD=Q6581097|1=Male (gender)}} | venerated_in = {{ill|Shinto|WD=Q812767|1=Shinto}} }} '''Sukunabikona''' or '''Sukuna bikona''' (少彦名神, also known as '''Sukuna-biko''', '''Sukuna-biko-na''', '''Sukuna hikona''') is the [[Shinto]] [[kami]] of the ''{{ill|Onsen|en|Onsen|WD=Q655311|lt=Onsen|1=Onsen}}'' ({{ill|Hot spring|en|Hot spring|WD=Q177380|lt=Hot spring|1=Hot spring}}), agriculture, healing, magic, brewing [[:en:Sake (beverage)|sake]] and knowledge. His name means "the small lord of renown." He is often described as being a {{ill|Dwarf (folklore)|en|Dwarf (folklore)|WD=Q214045|lt=dwarf|1=Dwarf (folklore)}} and is frequently paired with [[Ōkuninushi]].<ref name="auto">Handbook of Japanese Mythology by Michael Ashkenazi, ABC-CLIO, 2003</ref>
==Adventures with Ōkuninushi==
One day while Ōkuninushi was at {{ill|Miho Bay|en|Miho Bay|WD=Q6845312|lt=Miho Bay|1=Miho Bay}}, he saw a small boat on the {{ill|Wind wave|en|Wind wave|WD=Q165848|lt=whitecap waves|1=Wind wave}}. The boat was made of a {{ill|Metaplexis|en|Metaplexis|WD=Q11692226|lt=Metaplexis|1=Metaplexis}} pod. Inside was a small dwarf, no bigger than a thumb. Ōkuninushi picked him up, and Sukuna-biko-na bit him on the cheek. Ōkuninushi asked him his name, but he would not reply. Then a nearby toad said to bring Sukuna-biko-na to {{ill|Kuebiko|en|Kuebiko|WD=Q6441847|lt=Kuebiko|1=Kuebiko}} the kami of agriculture, as the {{ill|Scarecrow|en|Scarecrow|WD=Q1990797|lt=Scarecrow|1=Scarecrow}} god would know. When Kuebiko saw the dwarf, he said "That is Sukuna son of Kami-Musubi." [[Kamimusubi]] is part of the primordial creator trinity with [[Takamimusubi]] and [[Ame-no-Minakanushi]]. Ōkuninushi was shocked that such a small being could be the son of such a powerful [[:en:Creator goddess|Creator goddess]].
Ōkuninushi brought Sukuna-biko-na to Kami-Musubi, who said that yes, he was her son, and that he fell out of her fingers. Ōkuninushi was on a quest to continue the creation of the {{ill|Names of Japan|en|Names of Japan|WD=Q13423964|lt=Central Reed Plain|1=Names of Japan}} and unite the people. She decreed that the two would work together to help finish building {{ill|Izumo Province|en|Izumo Province|WD=Q582846|lt=Izumo|1=Izumo Province}}, and they joined forces to help build the land.<ref>Kojiki edited by Donald L. Philippi, Princeton University Press, 2015. {{ISBN|9780691622071}}</ref>
Tradition holds that Sukuna-biko-na left this world at {{ill|Awaji Island|en|Awaji Island|WD=Q504373|lt=Awaji Island|1=Awaji Island}} by climbing a grain of {{ill|Millet|en|Millet|WD=Q259438|lt=Millet|1=Millet}}, which then dipped under his weight and rebounded, flinging him into {{ill|Ne-no-kuni|en|Ne-no-kuni|WD=Q7555425|lt=Tokoyo no Kuni|1=Ne-no-kuni}}.<ref name="auto2">Shinto, the Way of the Gods by William George Aston, Longmans Green, 1905</ref>
As part of his quest to help Ōkuninushi complete construction of the land, Sukuna-biko-na invented medicines and cures for illnesses and diseases, including magical spells for protection. In addition to his other domains, he is a master of magic and wizardry. His ascension into space, rather than a natural death, makes him a {{ill|Marebito|en|Marebito|WD=Q6758746|lt=Marebito|1=Marebito}}.<ref name="auto"/>
==As Deity of Hot Springs== On one of their adventures, Sukuna became ill. They went to {{ill|Dōgo Onsen|en|Dōgo Onsen|WD=Q1037748|lt=the Dōgo hot springs|1=Dōgo Onsen}}. His footprints left an impression on the rock, known as Tamanoishi, which remains in Dogo Onsen today north of the main building. <ref>{{Cite web|url=https://taiken.co/single/spirited-away-at-dogo-onsen/|title=Spirited Away at Dogo Onsen|website=Taiken Japan|access-date=2019-09-24|archive-date=2020-10-22|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201022042410/https://taiken.co/single/spirited-away-at-dogo-onsen/|url-status=dead}}</ref><ref name="auto3">A Handbook for Travellers in Japan, including the Whole Empire from Yezo to Formosa, by Basil Hall Chamberlain & W.B. Mason, 1901</ref> The ability for the natural hot spring to cure a god bestowed the waters, and the land itself from which they sprung, a divine importance.
He is known as an immigrant deity, in that he came from across the sea and then departed the land.<ref name="auto4"> Shotoku: Ethnicity, Ritual, and Violence in the Japanese Buddhist Tradition by Michael I. Como, Oxford University Press, 2008</ref>
==As Inventor of Sake== Some sources credit him as well with the invention of [[Sake]]. <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ca:Sukunabikona]][[co:commons:Category:Sukunabikona]][[en:Sukunabikona]][[fr:Sukunabikona]][[it:Sukuna-hikona]][[ja:スクナビコナ]][[ko:스쿠나비코나]][[mni:ꯁꯨꯀꯨꯅꯕꯤꯀꯣꯅ]][[pl:Sukunabikona]][[pt:Sukunahikona]][[simple:Sukunabikona]][[sv:Suku-Na-Bikona]][[zh:少彥名]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ca:Sukunabikona]][[co:commons:Category:Sukunabikona]][[en:Sukunabikona]][[fr:Sukunabikona]][[it:Sukuna-hikona]][[ja:スクナビコナ]][[ko:스쿠나비코나]][[mni:ꯁꯨꯀꯨꯅꯕꯤꯀꯣꯅ]][[pl:Sukunabikona]][[pt:Sukunahikona]][[simple:Sukunabikona]][[sv:Suku-Na-Bikona]][[zh:少彥名]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ca:Sukunabikona]][[Category:sn_crud|TESTcommons:Category:Sukunabikona]][[en:Sukunabikona]][[fr:Sukunabikona]][[it:Sukuna-hikona]][[ja:スクナビコナ]][[ko:스쿠나비코나]][[mni:ꯁꯨꯀꯨꯅꯕꯤꯀꯣꯅ]][[pl:Sukunabikona]][[pt:Sukunahikona]][[simple:Sukunabikona]][[sv:Suku-Na-Bikona]][[zh:少彥名]]
{{short description|Kami of hot springs (onsen), agriculture, healing, magic and sake}} {{Infobox deity | type = Shinto | name = Sukunabikona | image =Sukuna-biko and Okuninushi.jpg; File:Sukuna-biko and Okuninushi.jpg| parents =[[Kamimusubi]]; {{ill|Takamimusubi|WD=Q125400|1=Takamimusubi}}| gender = {{ill|male|WD=Q6581097|1=Male (gender)}} | venerated_in = {{ill|Shinto|WD=Q812767|1=Shinto}} }} '''Sukunabikona''' or '''Sukuna bikona''' (少彦名神, also known as '''Sukuna-biko''', '''Sukuna-biko-na''', '''Sukuna hikona''') is the [[Shinto]] [[kami]] of the ''{{ill|Onsen|en|Onsen|WD=Q655311|lt=Onsen|1=Onsen}}'' ({{ill|Hot spring|en|Hot spring|WD=Q177380|lt=Hot spring|1=Hot spring}}), agriculture, healing, magic, brewing [[:en:Sake (beverage)|sake]] and knowledge. His name means "the small lord of renown." He is often described as being a {{ill|Dwarf (folklore)|en|Dwarf (folklore)|WD=Q214045|lt=dwarf|1=Dwarf (folklore)}} and is frequently paired with [[Ōkuninushi]].<ref name="auto">Handbook of Japanese Mythology by Michael Ashkenazi, ABC-CLIO, 2003</ref>
==Adventures with Ōkuninushi==
One day while Ōkuninushi was at {{ill|Miho Bay|en|Miho Bay|WD=Q6845312|lt=Miho Bay|1=Miho Bay}}, he saw a small boat on the {{ill|Wind wave|en|Wind wave|WD=Q165848|lt=whitecap waves|1=Wind wave}}. The boat was made of a {{ill|Metaplexis|en|Metaplexis|WD=Q11692226|lt=Metaplexis|1=Metaplexis}} pod. Inside was a small dwarf, no bigger than a thumb. Ōkuninushi picked him up, and Sukuna-biko-na bit him on the cheek. Ōkuninushi asked him his name, but he would not reply. Then a nearby toad said to bring Sukuna-biko-na to {{ill|Kuebiko|en|Kuebiko|WD=Q6441847|lt=Kuebiko|1=Kuebiko}} the kami of agriculture, as the {{ill|Scarecrow|en|Scarecrow|WD=Q1990797|lt=Scarecrow|1=Scarecrow}} god would know. When Kuebiko saw the dwarf, he said "That is Sukuna son of Kami-Musubi." [[Kamimusubi]] is part of the primordial creator trinity with [[Takamimusubi]] and [[Ame-no-Minakanushi]]. Ōkuninushi was shocked that such a small being could be the son of such a powerful [[:en:Creator goddess|Creator goddess]].
Ōkuninushi brought Sukuna-biko-na to Kami-Musubi, who said that yes, he was her son, and that he fell out of her fingers. Ōkuninushi was on a quest to continue the creation of the {{ill|Names of Japan|en|Names of Japan|WD=Q13423964|lt=Central Reed Plain|1=Names of Japan}} and unite the people. She decreed that the two would work together to help finish building {{ill|Izumo Province|en|Izumo Province|WD=Q582846|lt=Izumo|1=Izumo Province}}, and they joined forces to help build the land.<ref>Kojiki edited by Donald L. Philippi, Princeton University Press, 2015. {{ISBN|9780691622071}}</ref>
Tradition holds that Sukuna-biko-na left this world at {{ill|Awaji Island|en|Awaji Island|WD=Q504373|lt=Awaji Island|1=Awaji Island}} by climbing a grain of {{ill|Millet|en|Millet|WD=Q259438|lt=Millet|1=Millet}}, which then dipped under his weight and rebounded, flinging him into {{ill|Ne-no-kuni|en|Ne-no-kuni|WD=Q7555425|lt=Tokoyo no Kuni|1=Ne-no-kuni}}.<ref name="auto2">Shinto, the Way of the Gods by William George Aston, Longmans Green, 1905</ref>
As part of his quest to help Ōkuninushi complete construction of the land, Sukuna-biko-na invented medicines and cures for illnesses and diseases, including magical spells for protection. In addition to his other domains, he is a master of magic and wizardry. His ascension into space, rather than a natural death, makes him a {{ill|Marebito|en|Marebito|WD=Q6758746|lt=Marebito|1=Marebito}}.<ref name="auto"/>
==As Deity of Hot Springs== On one of their adventures, Sukuna became ill. They went to {{ill|Dōgo Onsen|en|Dōgo Onsen|WD=Q1037748|lt=the Dōgo hot springs|1=Dōgo Onsen}}. Ōkuninushi put Sukuna-biko in the hot spring water to revive him. Sukuna hikona then took a nap. When he awoke, he was cured, and Sukuna danced on a stone. His footprints left an impression on the rock, known as Tamanoishi, which remains in Dogo Onsen today north of the main building. [[File:Dōgo_Onsen_in_2010-9-6_No,9.JPG|thumb|Tamanoishi at Dogo Onsen]]
<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://taiken.co/single/spirited-away-at-dogo-onsen/|title=Spirited Away at Dogo Onsen|website=Taiken Japan|access-date=2019-09-24|archive-date=2020-10-22|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201022042410/https://taiken.co/single/spirited-away-at-dogo-onsen/|url-status=dead}}</ref><ref name="auto3">A Handbook for Travellers in Japan, including the Whole Empire from Yezo to Formosa, by Basil Hall Chamberlain & W.B. Mason, 1901</ref> The ability for the natural hot spring to cure a god bestowed the waters, and the land itself from which they sprung, a divine importance.
He is known as an immigrant deity, in that he came from across the sea and then departed the land.<ref name="auto4"> Shotoku: Ethnicity, Ritual, and Violence in the Japanese Buddhist Tradition by Michael I. Como, Oxford University Press, 2008</ref>
==As Inventor of Sake== Some sources credit him as well with the invention of [[Sake]]. He taught the people how to brew the beverage from {{ill|Rice|en|Rice|WD=Q5090|lt=Rice|1=Rice}}.<ref>A History of the Japanese People from the Earliest Times to the End of the Meiji Era by Francis Brinkley and Kikuchi Dairoku, 1912</ref>
In the ''Kojiki'', {{ill|Empress Jingū|en|Empress Jingū|WD=Q232803|lt=Empress Jingū|1=Empress Jingū}} gives a banquet toast crediting the deity Sukuna with the creation of Sake:
<poem> This fine liquor Is not my fine liquor. The potion-master Who dwells in the Everworld (tokoyo) The rock-standing Deity Sukuna With godly blessing, Blessing in frenzy, With abundant blessing Blessing around and around Came and offered This fine liquor: Drink unsparingly ''Sa! Sa!''
Thereupon Takeuchi no Sukune no Mikoto replied on behalf of the prince:
He who brewed This fine liquor-was it because He took his drum And standing it up like a mortar, Singing while he brewed Did the brewing of the beer Dancing while he brewed Did the brewing of the beer, That this fine liquor, Fine liquor, Makes me feel so extra good? ''Sa! Sa!'' </poem>
Due to Sukuna bikona's gift of liquor to the people, he is sometimes paired with the goddess of food {{ill|Ukemochi|en|Ukemochi|WD=Q2280323|lt=Ukemochi|1=Ukemochi}}. The reference to the standing stone in the song refers to his worship as a sacred stone. Both Sukuna bikona and Ohona-mochi are enshrined as sacred stones in a temple in Noto Province.<ref name="auto2"/><ref name="auto4"/>
==Shrines== In Shinto, it is believed that the god resides within a sacred object housed at the shrine. It is possible for a deity to be in more than one place at a time, as such a Kami may be housed at many shrines, as well as being omnipresent and omniscient.
Sukuna Hikona is enshrined at, {{ill|Ōarai, Ibaraki|en|Ōarai, Ibaraki|WD=Q1349185|lt=Oarai Isosaki-jinja|1=Ōarai, Ibaraki}}. In the Montoku Jitsuroku, a book of Japanese history, Ōkuninushi and Sukuna Hikona descended in 856, proclaiming that they had returned to help the people of the land and a shrine was built to honor their arrival.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.pref.ibaraki.jp/bugai/kokusai/tabunka/en/oneday/20131220-oaraiisosaki.html|title=Oarai Isosaki Shrine|website=茨城県|access-date=2019-09-24|archive-date=2019-09-24|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190924223136/http://www.pref.ibaraki.jp/bugai/kokusai/tabunka/en/oneday/20131220-oaraiisosaki.html|url-status=dead}}</ref>
[[File:Sukunahikona-Shrine-Osaka.jpg|thumb|Sukunahikona shrine at Osaka]]
He is one of the Shinto deities enshrined at {{ill|Mount Mitake (Tokyo)|en|Mount Mitake (Tokyo)|WD=Q1432565|lt=Mt. Mitake|1=Mount Mitake (Tokyo)}}<ref name="auto3"/>
In Noto Province there are Stone Idols said to represent Sukuna bikona and Ohana-mochi.<ref name="auto2"/>
The [[少彦名神社 (大洲市)|Sukuna hikona shrine]] in Ozu city, Ehime prefecture was registered by the World Monument Foundation in 2014, "Monument List of the Endangered World." [[File:Sukunahikona_haiden.JPG|thumb|Sukunahikona haiden in Ozu city.]]
He is enshrined at Awashima in Wakayama. Empress Jingu, while on a journey over the sea, encountered a storm and her ship almost sank. She landed on Awashima island and discovered a shrine for Sukuna Hikona. She prayed for protection and promised she would build a larger shrine in his honor if he protected her. She was granted safe passage, and fulfilled her promise. Empress Jingu was then worshipped alongside Sukuna and Okuniushi. In one version, Harisaijo, Amaterasu's daughter, is exiled to the island of Awashima for having a woman's ailment. She encounters Sukuna hino's shrine and vows to help women.<ref>Encyclopedia of Spirits: The Ultimate Guide to the Magic of Fairies, Genies, Demons, Ghosts, Gods & Goddesses by Judika Illes, 2009, HarperOne First Edition</ref>
Empress Jingu is so taken by Sukuna Hikona that she carves a doll in his likeness, a hina-ningyō. This may be the origin of the Hina doll.<ref name="auto1">The Sea and the Sacred in Japan: Aspects of Maritime Religion by Fabio Rambelli, Bloomsbury Publishing 2018</ref>
==As Kami of Healing and Magic== Sukuna Hikona, as a kami of healing, is credited with the invention of cures for several diseases of humans and animals. He was invoked for protection against "creeping things" and "calamities." Creeping things refers to insects, snakes, and other pests that could damage crops. As a god of agriculture, farmers would pray to him for the protection of their crops. Calamities is a euphemism for unseen or spiritual forces. As a Marebito and a son of Kamimusubi, Sukuna bikona was omniscient and proficient in magic. He would offer protection in the way of spells and incantations.<ref>Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697, Volume 1</ref>
[[File:Sukunahikona-Shrine-Prayer-tablet.jpg|thumb|Prayer tablet (rear) of the Sukunahikona-Shrine in Osaka (Japan) showing the medicinal deities Sukunahikona (Japan) and Shennong (China)]]
For his interaction with Empress Jingu, who was pregnant at the time of her voyage, he is also credited with helping women's health, women's reproductive ailments, fertility, and safe childbirth.<ref name="auto1"/>
==Eponymy==
The asteroid {{ill|10725 Sukunabikona|en|10725 Sukunabikona|WD=Q2627958|lt=10725 Sukunabikona|1=10725 Sukunabikona}} derives its name from the kami.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://ssd.jpl.nasa.gov/sbdb.cgi?sstr=10725#content|title=JPL Small-Body Database Browser|website=ssd.jpl.nasa.gov}}</ref>
==References== {{Reflist}}
==External links== *{{Commonscatinline}}]]

{{short description|Kami of hot springs (onsen), agriculture, healing, magic and sake}} {{Infobox deity | type = Shinto | name = Sukunabikona | image =Sukuna-biko and Okuninushi.jpg; File:Sukuna-biko and Okuninushi.jpg| parents =[[Kamimusubi]]; {{ill|Takamimusubi|WD=Q125400|1=Takamimusubi}}| gender = {{ill|male|WD=Q6581097|1=Male (gender)}} | venerated_in = {{ill|Shinto|WD=Q812767|1=Shinto}} }} '''Sukunabikona''' or '''Sukuna bikona''' (少彦名神, also known as '''Sukuna-biko''', '''Sukuna-biko-na''', '''Sukuna hikona''') is the [[Shinto]] [[kami]] of the ''{{ill|Onsen|en|Onsen|WD=Q655311|lt=Onsen|1=Onsen}}'' ({{ill|Hot spring|en|Hot spring|WD=Q177380|lt=Hot spring|1=Hot spring}}), agriculture, healing, magic, brewing [[:en:Sake (beverage)|sake]] and knowledge. His name means "the small lord of renown." He is often described as being a {{ill|Dwarf (folklore)|en|Dwarf (folklore)|WD=Q214045|lt=dwarf|1=Dwarf (folklore)}} and is frequently paired with [[Ōkuninushi]].<ref name="auto">Handbook of Japanese Mythology by Michael Ashkenazi, ABC-CLIO, 2003</ref>
==Adventures with Ōkuninushi==
One day while Ōkuninushi was at {{ill|Miho Bay|en|Miho Bay|WD=Q6845312|lt=Miho Bay|1=Miho Bay}}, he saw a small boat on the {{ill|Wind wave|en|Wind wave|WD=Q165848|lt=whitecap waves|1=Wind wave}}. The boat was made of a {{ill|Metaplexis|en|Metaplexis|WD=Q11692226|lt=Metaplexis|1=Metaplexis}} pod. Inside was a small dwarf, no bigger than a thumb. Ōkuninushi picked him up, and Sukuna-biko-na bit him on the cheek. Ōkuninushi asked him his name, but he would not reply. Then a nearby toad said to bring Sukuna-biko-na to {{ill|Kuebiko|en|Kuebiko|WD=Q6441847|lt=Kuebiko|1=Kuebiko}} the kami of agriculture, as the {{ill|Scarecrow|en|Scarecrow|WD=Q1990797|lt=Scarecrow|1=Scarecrow}} god would know. When Kuebiko saw the dwarf, he said "That is Sukuna son of Kami-Musubi." [[Kamimusubi]] is part of the primordial creator trinity with [[Takamimusubi]] and [[Ame-no-Minakanushi]]. Ōkuninushi was shocked that such a small being could be the son of such a powerful [[:en:Creator goddess|Creator goddess]].
Ōkuninushi brought Sukuna-biko-na to Kami-Musubi, who said that yes, he was her son, and that he fell out of her fingers. Ōkuninushi was on a quest to continue the creation of the {{ill|Names of Japan|en|Names of Japan|WD=Q13423964|lt=Central Reed Plain|1=Names of Japan}} and unite the people. She decreed that the two would work together to help finish building {{ill|Izumo Province|en|Izumo Province|WD=Q582846|lt=Izumo|1=Izumo Province}}, and they joined forces to help build the land.<ref>Kojiki edited by Donald L. Philippi, Princeton University Press, 2015. {{ISBN|9780691622071}}</ref>
Tradition holds that Sukuna-biko-na left this world at {{ill|Awaji Island|en|Awaji Island|WD=Q504373|lt=Awaji Island|1=Awaji Island}} by climbing a grain of {{ill|Millet|en|Millet|WD=Q259438|lt=Millet|1=Millet}}, which then dipped under his weight and rebounded, flinging him into {{ill|Ne-no-kuni|en|Ne-no-kuni|WD=Q7555425|lt=Tokoyo no Kuni|1=Ne-no-kuni}}.<ref name="auto2">Shinto, the Way of the Gods by William George Aston, Longmans Green, 1905</ref>
As part of his quest to help Ōkuninushi complete construction of the land, Sukuna-biko-na invented medicines and cures for illnesses and diseases, including magical spells for protection. In addition to his other domains, he is a master of magic and wizardry. His ascension into space, rather than a natural death, makes him a {{ill|Marebito|en|Marebito|WD=Q6758746|lt=Marebito|1=Marebito}}.<ref name="auto"/>
==As Deity of Hot Springs== On one of their adventures, Sukuna became ill. They went to {{ill|Dōgo Onsen|en|Dōgo Onsen|WD=Q1037748|lt=the Dōgo hot springs|1=Dōgo Onsen}}. Ōkuninushi put Sukuna-biko in the hot spring water to revive him. Sukuna hikona then took a nap. When he awoke, he was cured, and Sukuna danced on a stone. His footprints left an impression on the rock, known as Tamanoishi, which remains in Dogo Onsen today north of the main building. [[File:Dōgo_Onsen_in_2010-9-6_No,9.JPG|thumb|Tamanoishi at Dogo Onsen]]
<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://taiken.co/single/spirited-away-at-dogo-onsen/|title=Spirited Away at Dogo Onsen|website=Taiken Japan|access-date=2019-09-24|archive-date=2020-10-22|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201022042410/https://taiken.co/single/spirited-away-at-dogo-onsen/|url-status=dead}}</ref><ref name="auto3">A Handbook for Travellers in Japan, including the Whole Empire from Yezo to Formosa, by Basil Hall Chamberlain & W.B. Mason, 1901</ref> The ability for the natural hot spring to cure a god bestowed the waters, and the land itself from which they sprung, a divine importance.
He is known as an immigrant deity, in that he came from across the sea and then departed the land.<ref name="auto4"> Shotoku: Ethnicity, Ritual, and Violence in the Japanese Buddhist Tradition by Michael I. Como, Oxford University Press, 2008</ref>
==As Inventor of Sake== Some sources credit him as well with the invention of [[Sake]]. He taught the people how to brew the beverage from {{ill|Rice|en|Rice|WD=Q5090|lt=Rice|1=Rice}}.<ref>A History of the Japanese People from the Earliest Times to the End of the Meiji Era by Francis Brinkley and Kikuchi Dairoku, 1912</ref>
In the ''Kojiki'', {{ill|Empress Jingū|en|Empress Jingū|WD=Q232803|lt=Empress Jingū|1=Empress Jingū}} gives a banquet toast crediting the deity Sukuna with the creation of Sake:
<poem> This fine liquor Is not my fine liquor. The potion-master Who dwells in the Everworld (tokoyo) The rock-standing Deity Sukuna With godly blessing, Blessing in frenzy, With abundant blessing Blessing around and around Came and offered This fine liquor: Drink unsparingly ''Sa! Sa!''
Thereupon Takeuchi no Sukune no Mikoto replied on behalf of the prince:
He who brewed This fine liquor-was it because He took his drum And standing it up like a mortar, Singing while he brewed Did the brewing of the beer Dancing while he brewed Did the brewing of the beer, That this fine liquor, Fine liquor, Makes me feel so extra good? ''Sa! Sa!'' </poem>
Due to Sukuna bikona's gift of liquor to the people, he is sometimes paired with the goddess of food {{ill|Ukemochi|en|Ukemochi|WD=Q2280323|lt=Ukemochi|1=Ukemochi}}. The reference to the standing stone in the song refers to his worship as a sacred stone. Both Sukuna bikona and Ohona-mochi are enshrined as sacred stones in a temple in Noto Province.<ref name="auto2"/><ref name="auto4"/>

Sukuna Hikona is enshrined at, {{ill|Ōarai, Ibaraki|en|Ōarai, Ibaraki|WD=Q1349185|lt=Oarai Isosaki-jinja|1=Ōarai, Ibaraki}}. In the Montoku Jitsuroku, a book of Japanese history, Ōkuninushi and Sukuna Hikona descended in 856, proclaiming that they had returned to help the people of the land and a shrine was built to honor their arrival.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.pref.ibaraki.jp/bugai/kokusai/tabunka/en/oneday/20131220-oaraiisosaki.html|title=Oarai Isosaki Shrine|website=茨城県|access-date=2019-09-24|archive-date=2019-09-24|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190924223136/http://www.pref.ibaraki.jp/bugai/kokusai/tabunka/en/oneday/20131220-oaraiisosaki.html|url-status=dead}}</ref>
[[File:Sukunahikona-Shrine-Osaka.jpg|thumb|Sukunahikona shrine at Osaka]]
He is one of the Shinto deities enshrined at {{ill|Mount Mitake (Tokyo)|en|Mount Mitake (Tokyo)|WD=Q1432565|lt=Mt. Mitake|1=Mount Mitake (Tokyo)}}<ref name="auto3"/>
In Noto Province there are Stone Idols said to represent Sukuna bikona and Ohana-mochi.<ref name="auto2"/>
The [[少彦名神社 (大洲市)|Sukuna hikona shrine]] in Ozu city, Ehime prefecture was registered by the World Monument Foundation in 2014, "Monument List of the Endangered World." [[File:Sukunahikona_haiden.JPG|thumb|Sukunahikona haiden in Ozu city.]]
He is enshrined at Awashima in Wakayama. Empress Jingu, while on a journey over the sea, encountered a storm and her ship almost sank. She landed on Awashima island and discovered a shrine for Sukuna Hikona. She prayed for protection and promised she would build a larger shrine in his honor if he protected her. She was granted safe passage, and fulfilled her promise. Empress Jingu was then worshipped alongside Sukuna and Okuniushi. In one version, Harisaijo, Amaterasu's daughter, is exiled to the island of Awashima for having a woman's ailment. She encounters Sukuna hino's shrine and vows to help women.<ref>Encyclopedia of Spirits: The Ultimate Guide to the Magic of Fairies, Genies, Demons, Ghosts, Gods & Goddesses by Judika Illes, 2009, HarperOne First Edition</ref>
Empress Jingu is so taken by Sukuna Hikona that she carves a doll in his likeness, a hina-ningyō. This may be the origin of the Hina doll.<ref name="auto1">The Sea and the Sacred in Japan: Aspects of Maritime Religion by Fabio Rambelli, Bloomsbury Publishing 2018</ref>
==As Kami of Healing and Magic== Sukuna Hikona, as a kami of healing, is credited with the invention of cures for several diseases of humans and animals. He was invoked for protection against "creeping things" and "calamities." Creeping things refers to insects, snakes, and other pests that could damage crops. As a god of agriculture, farmers would pray to him for the protection of their crops. Calamities is a euphemism for unseen or spiritual forces. As a Marebito and a son of Kamimusubi, Sukuna bikona was omniscient and proficient in magic. He would offer protection in the way of spells and incantations.<ref>Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697, Volume 1</ref>
[[File:Sukunahikona-Shrine-Prayer-tablet.jpg|thumb|Prayer tablet (rear) of the Sukunahikona-Shrine in Osaka (Japan) showing the medicinal deities Sukunahikona (Japan) and Shennong (China)]]
For his interaction with Empress Jingu, who was pregnant at the time of her voyage, he is also credited with helping women's health, women's reproductive ailments, fertility, and safe childbirth.<ref name="auto1"/>
==Eponymy==
The asteroid {{ill|10725 Sukunabikona|en|10725 Sukunabikona|WD=Q2627958|lt=10725 Sukunabikona|1=10725 Sukunabikona}} derives its name from the kami.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://ssd.jpl.nasa.gov/sbdb.cgi?sstr=10725#content|title=JPL Small-Body Database Browser|website=ssd.jpl.nasa.gov}}</ref>
==References== {{Reflist}}
==External links== *{{Commonscatinline}}]]

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ca:Sukunabikona]][[co:commons:Category:Sukunabikona]][[en:Sukunabikona]][[fr:Sukunabikona]][[it:Sukuna-hikona]][[ja:スクナビコナ]][[ko:스쿠나비코나]][[mni:ꯁꯨꯀꯨꯅꯕꯤꯀꯣꯅ]][[pl:Sukunabikona]][[pt:Sukunahikona]][[simple:Sukunabikona]][[sv:Suku-Na-Bikona]][[zh:少彥名]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ca:Sukunabikona]][[co:commons:Category:Sukunabikona]][[en:Sukunabikona]][[fr:Sukunabikona]][[it:Sukuna-hikona]][[ja:スクナビコナ]][[ko:스쿠나비코나]][[mni:ꯁꯨꯀꯨꯅꯕꯤꯀꯣꯅ]][[pl:Sukunabikona]][[pt:Sukunahikona]][[simple:Sukunabikona]][[sv:Suku-Na-Bikona]][[zh:少彥名]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ca:Sukunabikona]][[Category:sn_crud|TESTcommons:Category:Sukunabikona]][[en:Sukunabikona]][[fr:Sukunabikona]][[it:Sukuna-hikona]][[ja:スクナビコナ]][[ko:스쿠나비코나]][[mni:ꯁꯨꯀꯨꯅꯕꯤꯀꯣꯅ]][[pl:Sukunabikona]][[pt:Sukunahikona]][[simple:Sukunabikona]][[sv:Suku-Na-Bikona]][[zh:少彥名]]
{{short description|Kami of hot springs (onsen), agriculture, healing, magic and sake}} {{Infobox deity | type = Shinto | name = Sukunabikona | image =Sukuna-biko and Okuninushi.jpg; File:Sukuna-biko and Okuninushi.jpg| parents =[[Kamimusubi]]; {{ill|Takamimusubi|WD=Q125400|1=Takamimusubi}}| gender = {{ill|male|WD=Q6581097|1=Male (gender)}} | venerated_in = {{ill|Shinto|WD=Q812767|1=Shinto}} }} '''Sukunabikona''' or '''Sukuna bikona''' (少彦名神, also known as '''Sukuna-biko''', '''Sukuna-biko-na''', '''Sukuna hikona''') is the [[Shinto]] [[kami]] of the ''{{ill|Onsen|en|Onsen|WD=Q655311|lt=Onsen|1=Onsen}}'' ({{ill|Hot spring|en|Hot spring|WD=Q177380|lt=Hot spring|1=Hot spring}}), agriculture, healing, magic, brewing [[:en:Sake (beverage)|sake]] and knowledge. His name means "the small lord of renown." He is often described as being a {{ill|Dwarf (folklore)|en|Dwarf (folklore)|WD=Q214045|lt=dwarf|1=Dwarf (folklore)}} and is frequently paired with [[Ōkuninushi]].<ref name="auto">Handbook of Japanese Mythology by Michael Ashkenazi, ABC-CLIO, 2003</ref>
==Adventures with Ōkuninushi==
One day while Ōkuninushi was at {{ill|Miho Bay|en|Miho Bay|WD=Q6845312|lt=Miho Bay|1=Miho Bay}}, he saw a small boat on the {{ill|Wind wave|en|Wind wave|WD=Q165848|lt=whitecap waves|1=Wind wave}}. The boat was made of a {{ill|Metaplexis|en|Metaplexis|WD=Q11692226|lt=Metaplexis|1=Metaplexis}} pod. Inside was a small dwarf, no bigger than a thumb. Ōkuninushi picked him up, and Sukuna-biko-na bit him on the cheek. Ōkuninushi asked him his name, but he would not reply. Then a nearby toad said to bring Sukuna-biko-na to {{ill|Kuebiko|en|Kuebiko|WD=Q6441847|lt=Kuebiko|1=Kuebiko}} the kami of agriculture, as the {{ill|Scarecrow|en|Scarecrow|WD=Q1990797|lt=Scarecrow|1=Scarecrow}} god would know. When Kuebiko saw the dwarf, he said "That is Sukuna son of Kami-Musubi." [[Kamimusubi]] is part of the primordial creator trinity with [[Takamimusubi]] and [[Ame-no-Minakanushi]]. Ōkuninushi was shocked that such a small being could be the son of such a powerful [[:en:Creator goddess|Creator goddess]].
Ōkuninushi brought Sukuna-biko-na to Kami-Musubi, who said that yes, he was her son, and that he fell out of her fingers. Ōkuninushi was on a quest to continue the creation of the {{ill|Names of Japan|en|Names of Japan|WD=Q13423964|lt=Central Reed Plain|1=Names of Japan}} and unite the people. She decreed that the two would work together to help finish building {{ill|Izumo Province|en|Izumo Province|WD=Q582846|lt=Izumo|1=Izumo Province}}, and they joined forces to help build the land.<ref>Kojiki edited by Donald L. Philippi, Princeton University Press, 2015. {{ISBN|9780691622071}}</ref>
Tradition holds that Sukuna-biko-na left this world at {{ill|Awaji Island|en|Awaji Island|WD=Q504373|lt=Awaji Island|1=Awaji Island}} by climbing a grain of {{ill|Millet|en|Millet|WD=Q259438|lt=Millet|1=Millet}}, which then dipped under his weight and rebounded, flinging him into {{ill|Ne-no-kuni|en|Ne-no-kuni|WD=Q7555425|lt=Tokoyo no Kuni|1=Ne-no-kuni}}.<ref name="auto2">Shinto, the Way of the Gods by William George Aston, Longmans Green, 1905</ref>
As part of his quest to help Ōkuninushi complete construction of the land, Sukuna-biko-na invented medicines and cures for illnesses and diseases, including magical spells for protection. In addition to his other domains, he is a master of magic and wizardry. His ascension into space, rather than a natural death, makes him a {{ill|Marebito|en|Marebito|WD=Q6758746|lt=Marebito|1=Marebito}}.<ref name="auto"/>
==As Deity of Hot Springs== On one of their adventures, Sukuna became ill. They went to {{ill|Dōgo Onsen|en|Dōgo Onsen|WD=Q1037748|lt=the Dōgo hot springs|1=Dōgo Onsen}}. Ōkuninushi put Sukuna-biko in the hot spring water to revive him. Sukuna hikona then took a nap. When he awoke, he was cured, and Sukuna danced on a stone. His footprints left an impression on the rock, known as Tamanoishi, which remains in Dogo Onsen today north of the main building. [[File:Dōgo_Onsen_in_2010-9-6_No,9.JPG|thumb|Tamanoishi at Dogo Onsen]]
<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://taiken.co/single/spirited-away-at-dogo-onsen/|title=Spirited Away at Dogo Onsen|website=Taiken Japan|access-date=2019-09-24|archive-date=2020-10-22|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201022042410/https://taiken.co/single/spirited-away-at-dogo-onsen/|url-status=dead}}</ref><ref name="auto3">A Handbook for Travellers in Japan, including the Whole Empire from Yezo to Formosa, by Basil Hall Chamberlain & W.B. Mason, 1901</ref> The ability for the natural hot spring to cure a god bestowed the waters, and the land itself from which they sprung, a divine importance.
He is known as an immigrant deity, in that he came from across the sea and then departed the land.<ref name="auto4"> Shotoku: Ethnicity, Ritual, and Violence in the Japanese Buddhist Tradition by Michael I. Como, Oxford University Press, 2008</ref>
==As Inventor of Sake== Some sources credit him as well with the invention of [[Sake]]. He taught the people how to brew the beverage from {{ill|Rice|en|Rice|WD=Q5090|lt=Rice|1=Rice}}.<ref>A History of the Japanese People from the Earliest Times to the End of the Meiji Era by Francis Brinkley and Kikuchi Dairoku, 1912</ref>
In the ''Kojiki'', {{ill|Empress Jingū|en|Empress Jingū|WD=Q232803|lt=Empress Jingū|1=Empress Jingū}} gives a banquet toast crediting the deity Sukuna with the creation of Sake:
<poem> This fine liquor Is not my fine liquor. The potion-master Who dwells in the Everworld (tokoyo) The rock-standing Deity Sukuna With godly blessing, Blessing in frenzy, With abundant blessing Blessing around and around Came and offered This fine liquor: Drink unsparingly ''Sa! Sa!''
Thereupon Takeuchi no Sukune no Mikoto replied on behalf of the prince:
He who brewed This fine liquor-was it because He took his drum And standing it up like a mortar, Singing while he brewed Did the brewing of the beer Dancing while he brewed Did the brewing of the beer, That this fine liquor, Fine liquor, Makes me feel so extra good? ''Sa! Sa!'' </poem>
Due to Sukuna bikona's gift of liquor to the people, he is sometimes paired with the goddess of food {{ill|Ukemochi|en|Ukemochi|WD=Q2280323|lt=Ukemochi|1=Ukemochi}}. The reference to the standing stone in the song refers to his worship as a sacred stone. Both Sukuna bikona and Ohona-mochi are enshrined as sacred stones in a temple in Noto Province.<ref name="auto2"/><ref name="auto4"/>
==Shrines== In Shinto, it is believed that the god resides within a sacred object housed at the shrine. It is possible for a deity to be in more than one place at a time, as such a Kami may be housed at many shrines, as well as being omnipresent and omniscient.
Sukuna Hikona is enshrined at, {{ill|Ōarai, Ibaraki|en|Ōarai, Ibaraki|WD=Q1349185|lt=Oarai Isosaki-jinja|1=Ōarai, Ibaraki}}. In the Montoku Jitsuroku, a book of Japanese history, Ōkuninushi and Sukuna Hikona descended in 856, proclaiming that they had returned to help the people of the land and a shrine was built to honor their arrival.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.pref.ibaraki.jp/bugai/kokusai/tabunka/en/oneday/20131220-oaraiisosaki.html|title=Oarai Isosaki Shrine|website=茨城県|access-date=2019-09-24|archive-date=2019-09-24|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190924223136/http://www.pref.ibaraki.jp/bugai/kokusai/tabunka/en/oneday/20131220-oaraiisosaki.html|url-status=dead}}</ref>
[[File:Sukunahikona-Shrine-Osaka.jpg|thumb|Sukunahikona shrine at Osaka]]
He is one of the Shinto deities enshrined at {{ill|Mount Mitake (Tokyo)|en|Mount Mitake (Tokyo)|WD=Q1432565|lt=Mt. Mitake|1=Mount Mitake (Tokyo)}}<ref name="auto3"/>
In Noto Province there are Stone Idols said to represent Sukuna bikona and Ohana-mochi.<ref name="auto2"/>
The [[少彦名神社 (大洲市)|Sukuna hikona shrine]] in Ozu city, Ehime prefecture was registered by the World Monument Foundation in 2014, "Monument List of the Endangered World." [[File:Sukunahikona_haiden.JPG|thumb|Sukunahikona haiden in Ozu city.]]
He is enshrined at Awashima in Wakayama. Empress Jingu, while on a journey over the sea, encountered a storm and her ship almost sank. She landed on Awashima island and discovered a shrine for Sukuna Hikona. She prayed for protection and promised she would build a larger shrine in his honor if he protected her. She was granted safe passage, and fulfilled her promise. Empress Jingu was then worshipped alongside Sukuna and Okuniushi. In one version, Harisaijo, Amaterasu's daughter, is exiled to the island of Awashima for having a woman's ailment. She encounters Sukuna hino's shrine and vows to help women.<ref>Encyclopedia of Spirits: The Ultimate Guide to the Magic of Fairies, Genies, Demons, Ghosts, Gods & Goddesses by Judika Illes, 2009, HarperOne First Edition</ref>
Empress Jingu is so taken by Sukuna Hikona that she carves a doll in his likeness, a hina-ningyō. This may be the origin of the Hina doll.<ref name="auto1">The Sea and the Sacred in Japan: Aspects of Maritime Religion by Fabio Rambelli, Bloomsbury Publishing 2018</ref>
==As Kami of Healing and Magic== Sukuna Hikona, as a kami of healing, is credited with the invention of cures for several diseases of humans and animals. He was invoked for protection against "creeping things" and "calamities." Creeping things refers to insects, snakes, and other pests that could damage crops. As a god of agriculture, farmers would pray to him for the protection of their crops. Calamities is a euphemism for unseen or spiritual forces. As a Marebito and a son of Kamimusubi, Sukuna bikona was omniscient and proficient in magic. He would offer protection in the way of spells and incantations.<ref>Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697, Volume 1</ref>
[[File:Sukunahikona-Shrine-Prayer-tablet.jpg|thumb|Prayer tablet (rear) of the Sukunahikona-Shrine in Osaka (Japan) showing the medicinal deities Sukunahikona (Japan) and Shennong (China)]]
For his interaction with Empress Jingu, who was pregnant at the time of her voyage, he is also credited with helping women's health, women's reproductive ailments, fertility, and safe childbirth.<ref name="auto1"/>
==Eponymy==
The asteroid {{ill|10725 Sukunabikona|en|10725 Sukunabikona|WD=Q2627958|lt=10725 Sukunabikona|1=10725 Sukunabikona}} derives its name from the kami.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://ssd.jpl.nasa.gov/sbdb.cgi?sstr=10725#content|title=JPL Small-Body Database Browser|website=ssd.jpl.nasa.gov}}</ref>
==References== {{Reflist}}
